description: Execute a plan until it is complete

content: Please read AGENTS.md and follow its instructions. Then delete `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` if it already exists so this flow cannot inherit stale active-plan state from an earlier run. Do not fail if the file is missing, and do not create a replacement in this step. If you deleted it, commit the change and then, if you can, push.

Now you know the selected plan/spec/story, write or overwrite `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` with only the selected relative plan path using the shape `{ "plan_path": "planning/<story-file>.md" }`. Do not write absolute paths. This handoff file becomes the sole plan-selection source for every later step in this flow. Commit this file, and push if you can

Please read AGENTS.md and follow its instructions. Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Verify that the selected plan file exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated. Do NOT use code_info tools for plan selection and do NOT independently search for or select a different plan. Confirm to me what story that selected plan is, and provide me with an overview of the story, including what is still remaining to be done, without making file changes at this time.

Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Verify that the selected plan file exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated. Then check the last commit AND re-read that same plan, even if you have read it before. You MUST double-check to see if tasks have been added to it by another agent or if anything has changed. You must ensure you fully understand the plan and its remaining tasks before proceeding. You MUST read the plan and CANNOT assume you understand it from before. You MUST read the whole plan again even if you have read it before. Once you have done this, give me a summary of the remaining work.

Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Verify that the selected plan file exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated. Then explain the gotchas a developer would need to watch out for while implementing the next task, given all the information within the Description, its subsections, the AC, and the Out of Scope section. Also take into account the implementation information of any previous tasks. If you had asked for clarification about a blocker, then ensure you re-read the task, as it will have answers for you to proceed within the implementation notes of this task. No matter what, even if you think you know what is in the plan, you must re-read at least the next task, as it is possible changes have been made since you last read it. If the previous loop iteration involved a blocker or planner edits, you must also re-read the next downstream task that depends on the current task so you understand the updated sequencing and proof expectations.

Continue using only the relative `plan_path` stored in `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` as the active plan for this flow. Do not switch plans or rediscover the next story independently. Taking these gotchas into account, please fully work through the next task yourself, precisely following the steps within the 'Implementation Plan Instructions' section. You must keep the subtasks tick box updated as you work through each subtask to ensure everyone understands what has been done. Then run through the testing steps, ensuring you keep the subtasks tick box updated as you work through each testing step. Note that LMStudio is running and available, and Codex has been logged in locally and works. Get back to me once all subtasks and testing steps are complete. Get back to me once the task is FULLY complete AND each subtask is marked complete AND each testing step is marked complete, with a concise summary of what changed, the gotchas you came across while working through the task, and any commands or output that indicate success. Remember to mark each subtask and testing step as complete immediately after completing it rather than waiting until the end so your progress can be monitored. DO NOT BOTHER ME unless a blocker is hit or the whole task is complete and is marked as such. If a blocker is hit, write it into the implementation notes marked as **BLOCKER** and write a note describing the issue you hit. Blockers must include the exact subtask or testing step where work stopped, what you tried, the exact capability that is missing and whether you think the task should be split, re-ordered or re-written before work continues. Do not invent fake runtime seams, fake containers, fake health checks, or pretend a proof step passed when the required capability does not yet exist. Remember to keep your implementation notes concise and to the point. Remember that your normal output will not be seen by users or agents, and any issues MUST be marked as **BLOCKER** within the implementation notes to be visible to others to help you. YOU MUST commit, but not push, before finishing this request, no matter what changes you made.

Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Do not rediscover the story independently. Then audit the coding agent's work on that plan honestly: check whether completed work was implemented but left unmarked, and correct tasks, subtask and testing statuses if the evidence supports it. Identify any blocker notes marked **BLOCKER**. Do not stop at task status only. Capture exactly what remains incomplete and whether the blocker is local to the task or reveals a design problem within the plan. If you make tracked changes, you MUST commit them, but do not push.

steps.label: Research blocker solution and prove it

content: Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Do not rediscover the story independently. If the implementation does not contain a blocker, just state that. If the implementation notes contain a blocker, you MUST research and prove the solution before continuing. Your research must cover: 1. Pattern research - use the code_info tool to see how other ingested repositoires solve the same class of problem, and use deepwiki, context7 and web searches to confirm intended usage of the relevant external libraries and frameworks. Prefer existing repository and official library solutions over invented approaches. 2. Issue-resolution research - research the exact blocker or error itself using official docs and internet searches, and find how other engineers resolve the same concrete failure mode in practice. Distinguish between temporary workarounds, proper fixes, and anti-patterns. 3. You MUST prove the chosen solution before editing the plan: record the repository precidents you found, record the external library precidents you found, record the issue resolution references you found, explain why the chosen fix fits the current local repo state, and explain why the rejected alternatives were not suitable. write the answer into the implementation notes marked as **BLOCKING ANSWER**. Do not ask me questions or for clarification and do not offer me options - you must solve this one. If you make tracked changes, you MUST commit them, but do not push.

Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Do not rediscover the story independently. If the implementation does not contain a blocker, or didn't contain a blocker you just researched and attempted to fix, just state that. If there is/was a blocker, you must decide if the blocker proves the plan itself is wrong or incomplete. You MUST check if the blocker reveals any of the following: missing prerequisite tasks, a task that is too large and must be split, a task that assumes a runtime seam, readiness contract, dependency, test harness, environment mapping, or startup contract that does not yet exist, a task with testing steps that cannot honestly be run at the point the task is supposed to complete, or later tasks that now require renumbering or reference updates. If any of those are true, you MUST repair the story before continuing: insert explicit prerequisite tasks, narrow or re-write a blocked task so it depends only on already proven capabilities, renumber all downstream tasks, update all cross-references, note-file references, prerequisite references, and testing gates, make the new subtasks detailed enough for a junior engineer to follow without asking for help, ensure each testing section is runnable at the point that task completes and actually proves the intended work, and write what changed and WHY inside the task and implementation notes. If the blocker exists because the current task assumes a runtime seam, dependency, readiness surface, test harness, environment mapping, or startup contract that does not yet exist, you MUST prefer inserting explicit prerequisite tasks over repeatedly retrying the blocked task. If no story repair is needed, state explicitly why not. If you make tracked changes, you MUST commit them, but do not push.

Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and re-open the exact relative `plan_path` stored there after the planning agent step. Verify that the selected plan file still exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated. Confirm any Task Status updates or new tasks that were added, or any task renumbering that occured, and run `git status -sb` to verify the plan file change was committed. Then, identify the next executable task based on the updated plan, not based on what used to be blocked, and summarize the remaining tasks based on the updated plan before proceeding.

Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and open only the relative `plan_path` stored there. Do not rediscover the story independently. Before answering completion, list each task's Task Status (including the final task). Explicitly state whether the final task is __done__ or not, and cite the relative plan file path you read.

steps.label: Check for completion
steps.question: Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan. Verify that the selected plan file exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated. Then re-read that plan and check the last 3 git commits. You MUST confirm the final task's Task Status from the plan file and only answer yes if every task is marked __done__. Is the plan that was being worked on now complete? You MUST re-read the plan and check the last 3 git commits before giving your answer as the plan probably has changes made by other agents since you last looked at it. You must give only a yes or no answer. Reply as JSON {"answer":"yes"|"no"}.

content: Now that everything has been implemented, start a 3-step review sequence for the current story. This first step is an evidence gate only, not the findings pass and not the plan-editing pass. First read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Verify that the selected plan file exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated. Then re-read that active plan from disk, inspect the branch against `main`, and gather the evidence a PR reviewer would need before issuing findings. Repository review policy: treat `codeInfoStatus/**` as approved workflow artifacts, and treat `flows/**` plus `codex_agents/**` as approved workflow configuration. Changes under those paths must not be classified as suspicious, out-of-scope, or scope creep solely because they are absent from the active plan; instead, review them for workflow-contract correctness, artifact hygiene, path usage, and instruction/runtime safety. Required work: 1. Re-read the full active planning document and extract the Description, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, and the final completed tasks. 2. Inspect `git diff --name-status main...HEAD` and the recent branch commits. 3. Group changed files into planned implementation files, planned docs/tests/plan files, approved workflow artifacts, approved workflow configuration, and suspicious or out-of-scope files. 4. For every acceptance criterion, identify the current proof source: code path, tests, wrapper/test logs, screenshots/manual proof, or note that the proof is weak/missing. 5. Call out any implementation area that looks more complex or verbose than the planned work actually required, even if it may still be correct. 6. For each changed file or helper, record any review hotspots that the findings pass must inspect explicitly: merge-before-validate logic, normalization-before-validate logic, bootstrap or existence checks, helpers that return warnings/errors/reason metadata, shared log markers or shared response fields, fallback-selection logic, duplicate/conflicting object keys, deleted/moved/conditional validation, partial-failure handling, dead-field or dead-branch risk, any helper that could hide misconfiguration by defaulting too early, and any alias-migration or backward-compatibility helper where legacy and canonical fields can partially coexist in mixed-shape configs. 7. Identify any changed external contract surfaces that need explicit before/after comparison in findings: API routes, config file shapes, persisted artifacts, wrapper outputs, log marker/event schemas, and legacy alias/deprecated-input compatibility where old and new field shapes may coexist. Note where backward-compatibility risk exists and where the active plan explicitly permits an edge-case deviation from generic best practice. 8. Name the top 3 changed helpers/functions by review risk and record the worst malformed or contradictory input each one should reject or survive, plus whether that path currently has direct proof, indirect proof, or missing proof. 9. Generate a unique `review_pass_id` using the story number from the selected plan filename, a UTC timestamp, and the current HEAD short SHA. 10. Write the evidence summary to `codeInfoStatus/reviews/<review_pass_id>-evidence.md`. This evidence file is a durable review artifact that MUST be committed later so a human can inspect it after the story completes. 11. Write or overwrite a handoff file at `codeInfoStatus/reviews/<story-number>-current-review.json` containing at least: `story_id`, `plan_path`, `branch`, `base_branch`, `head_commit`, `review_pass_id`, `evidence_file`, and `findings_file` set to null. This handoff file is the ONLY review file the next step may use; do not rely on timestamps or 'latest file' discovery. Treat the handoff file as transient workflow state, not as the durable review artifact. 12. Do not edit the plan in this step unless a tiny note is absolutely required to unblock the review, and do not commit in this step unless you had to make tracked changes for that unblock. Report the evidence summary and the exact handoff file path when done.

Continue the current story review using ONLY the handoff file written by the previous review-evidence step. Do NOT discover the latest review artifact by timestamp. First read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Verify that the selected plan file exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated; do not edit the plan. Then read `codeInfoStatus/reviews/<story-number>-current-review.json`, derived from the selected plan filename, and verify that its `story_id` matches the current plan, its `branch` matches the current branch, its `head_commit` still matches the branch HEAD, and its referenced evidence file exists. If any of those checks fail, stop and say the review handoff is stale and must be regenerated; do not edit the plan. If the handoff is valid, perform the actual review against the planned work and the branch diff. Repository review policy: `codeInfoStatus/**`, `flows/**`, and `codex_agents/**` are approved workflow-support paths. Do not raise findings solely because those paths changed without being named in the active plan. Review `codeInfoStatus/**` for artifact hygiene, durable-vs-transient artifact handling, and workflow-contract correctness. Review `flows/**` and `codex_agents/**` for workflow semantics, instruction safety, stale-handoff handling, commit/push behavior, plan-selection rules, and other agent-control correctness. Required review areas: correctness against the story plan, acceptance criteria coverage, code quality, maintainability, performance, security, configuration/runtime correctness, documentation drift, scope creep, and whether the code is more verbose or complex than needed and could be made more succinct without sacrificing quality. After the plan-based review, you MUST perform a second pass that is not limited by the acceptance criteria and look for generic engineering defects in the changed code even if the active plan did not mention them. In that second pass, prioritize: invalid input being silently normalized into success, warnings/errors/reason values produced by helpers but dropped by callers, inconsistent schema or value vocabularies for the same log marker/event/response field across surfaces, bootstrap/existence checks that may misclassify files/directories/invalid paths, duplicate or conflicting object keys/payload fields, deleted/moved/conditional validation, partial-failure behavior, dead-field or dead-branch risk, and logic that hides misconfiguration by falling back too early. For every changed API route, config shape, persisted artifact, wrapper output, or shared log marker/event schema, perform a before/after contract comparison and state whether the change is backward compatible, intentionally breaking, or unclear. For every changed helper that merges, normalizes, or defaults config/runtime/user input before validation, verify that malformed input is not silently dropped, coerced into `{}`, or replaced by inherited defaults before validation can reject it. Also check whether validation was deleted, moved later, or made conditional in a way that weakens the previous contract. For any changed normalization, alias-migration, or backward-compatibility helper, compare old vs new behavior for mixed-shape inputs where legacy and canonical fields partially coexist. Flag regressions where a guard changed from checking whether the canonical field exists to checking only whether the parent object exists, or any similar narrowing that makes previously accepted configs stop working. For any changed function that returns warnings/errors/reason metadata or otherwise mixes data with diagnostics, trace each changed caller and state whether those diagnostics are surfaced to clients, logged, or intentionally dropped; if dropped, decide whether that is an intentional contract choice or a defect. If the same log marker, event name, or response field is emitted from more than one code path, compare the emitted schema and value vocabulary across those emitters and flag mismatches that would make downstream parsing, analytics, or operational debugging ambiguous. For fallback-selection logic, verify that precedence still matches the active plan and does not override explicit user intent. For partial-failure logic, verify what happens when only part of the resolution succeeds and whether the resulting behavior is explicit, safe, and observable. Before raising a finding about bootstrap, existence checks, or invalid-path handling, compare the implementation against the story's explicit edge cases or failure-mode contract and do not raise a finding solely because the code differs from a generic best practice if it matches the plan's stated contract. At minimum, inspect the top 3 changed helpers/functions by review risk from the evidence artifact and explicitly ask what malformed or contradictory input could still make each one behave incorrectly even if the current tests pass. State whether each risky path has direct proof, indirect proof, or missing proof, and raise a finding when a risky path is only protected by happy-path coverage or is otherwise weakly proven. Look for new fields that are written but never read, branches that cannot be reached under the current contract, and diagnostics that are intentionally hidden from clients without an actionable log trail. When a valid, low-risk consistency problem is found in files already changed by the story, and the fix does not change public payloads or otherwise broaden scope, prefer `should_fix` over `optional_simplification` so the cleanup is attempted rather than deferred by default. This step MUST produce findings only and MUST NOT edit the plan yet. Write the findings to `codeInfoStatus/reviews/<review_pass_id>-findings.md` using findings-first ordering by severity, with file references and explicit classification of each finding as `must_fix`, `should_fix`, or `optional_simplification`. Also state for each finding whether it is a `plan_contract_issue` or a `generic_engineering_issue`. This findings file is a durable review artifact that MUST be committed later so a human can inspect it after the story completes. If no findings exist, state that explicitly and also record any residual risks or weak-proof areas. Update the same handoff file so `findings_file` points to the exact findings artifact, and include any useful counts or disposition hints. Do not commit in this step unless you were forced to make tracked changes to repair the review artifacts themselves.

Finish the current story review using ONLY the handoff file and findings file referenced by that handoff. Do NOT rediscover review artifacts by timestamp. First read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan for this flow. Verify that the selected plan file exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated; do not edit the plan. Then read `codeInfoStatus/reviews/<story-number>-current-review.json`, derived from the selected plan filename, again and verify that its `story_id`, `branch`, `head_commit`, `review_pass_id`, `evidence_file`, and `findings_file` still match the current repo state. If the handoff is stale or incomplete, stop and say the review must be rerun; do not edit the plan. If the handoff is valid, decide what the plan must do with the findings. Repository review policy: treat `codeInfoStatus/**` as approved workflow artifacts, and treat `flows/**` plus `codex_agents/**` as approved workflow configuration. Do not reopen the story or record scope-creep findings solely because those paths changed without being named in the active plan. Only reopen if the review shows those changes introduced incorrect workflow behavior, broke repository contracts, or require explicit follow-up validation work. Rules: 1. If any `must_fix` or `should_fix` findings exist, reopen the story in the active plan, add a `Code Review Findings` summary section, add explicit follow-up tasks using the same structure as previous tasks, and add a fresh full re-test/final validation task after those review-fix tasks so the story must be revalidated against the acceptance criteria. Update numbering and cross-references if needed. 2. If only `optional_simplification` findings exist, reopen the story when the simplification is localized to files already changed by the story, low-risk, objectively testable, and improves a shared contract such as logging vocabulary, marker schema, or configuration consistency. Only defer it when the cleanup is speculative, broad, or not worth the churn; if it is deferred, record it in a short review note instead of reopening. 3. If there are no findings, append a `Post-Implementation Code Review` section to the end of the plan detailing the branch-vs-main checks performed, the acceptance-evidence checks performed, the files inspected, and why the story remains complete. 4. When the review is assessing the planned work, it MUST explicitly state whether each acceptance criterion has direct proof, indirect proof, or missing proof, and whether the implemented code is appropriately succinct for the required behavior or contains simplification opportunities. 5. The current pass `evidence_file` and `findings_file` are durable review artifacts and MUST be added to the commit alongside any plan changes so a human can inspect them later. The transient handoff file should not be treated as the durable artifact; once it has been consumed successfully, either remove it before committing or leave it untracked so later review passes do not rely on stale committed handoff state. 6. Commit the plan and the current pass review artifacts if you make tracked changes, but do not push. This is the only review step allowed to mutate the plan.

steps.label: Check for completion
steps.question: Read `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` first and use only the relative `plan_path` stored there as the active plan. Verify that the selected plan file exists and that the story number in the current branch name matches the story number in the selected plan filename. If those do not match, stop and say the current-plan handoff is stale and must be regenerated. Then re-read that plan and check the last 3 git commits. Is the plan that was being worked on now complete? You MUST re-read the plan and check the last 3 git commits before giving your answer, as the plan probably has changes made by other agents since you last looked at it. You must give only a yes or no answer. Reply as JSON {"answer":"yes"|"no"}.

content: The flow has now finished working through the selected story. Delete `codeInfoStatus/flow-state/current-plan.json` so later runs cannot reuse stale active-plan state. Do not fail if the file is already missing.
If possible, git push the branch.
